questions.text: When I'm upset, the feeling saturates my entire body.
I tend not to notice when two people share a meaningful look during a conversation.
I rarely sense when someone is tailoring their message based on who is listening.
I find that a calm, objective stance during others' crises allows me to be a stabilizing force when one is needed most.
I schedule regular maintenance for my car, home, and other possessions.
I prefer relationships where I set the agenda.
I find that analytical clarity during times of distress has made me a more strategic thinker and a more dependable partner in collaborative problem-solving.
I find that self-focus is what allows me to be consistent and reliable.
I find that my own ambitions are the primary engine of my daily motivation.
I welcome unexpected changes because they often lead somewhere more interesting.
I would rather live simply so I can contribute more to the people and causes around me.
I am drawn to situations where every voice carries equal weight.
I find satisfaction in helping someone else's vision come to life.
I notice that I rarely need to actively calm myself down — my system does it automatically.
I find that sadness in me is a gentle ache rather than a consuming force.
I intuitively understand the unspoken hierarchy in any social group I enter.
I resist routines because they make my days feel repetitive and dull.
I find that the felt weight of another person's suffering is the experience that has done the most to awaken my sense of moral urgency.
